ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for XO sauce. Mapped to the broader 'sauce (food product)' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A species-specific term for Cantonese dried-seafood condiment sauce does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Sauces and similar products) is the closest defensible category. No specific XO sauce code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for XO sauce or Cantonese seafood condiment sauce as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of XO sauce is approximately 15 grams (about 1 tablespoon), used as a condiment or flavoring agent for dim sum, stir-fries, or noodles.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sauce, fish, ready-to-serve (SR Legacy) and component-based estimation for dried scallop, dried shrimp, and chili-oil condiment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for XO sauce. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for dried scallops (FDC ID 175177), dried shrimp (FDC ID 175176), cured pork (ham), chili peppers, garlic, and vegetable oil, weighted by typical XO sauce formulation proportions. Input record values (protein 18.5 g, fat 38.5 g, carbohydrate 8.5 g, energy 450 kcal, sodium 1850 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this component-based estimation and are retained. Singapore HPB database does not carry an XO sauce entry. ICMR-NIN and IMR Malaysia MyFCD do not carry this item.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate condiment sauce — analogous to fish sauce and dried seafood condiments; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for XO sauce in the University of Sydney GI Database. XO sauce contains only 8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, of which the majority is from garlic and chili. The GI is estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate seafood-based condiments. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (15 × 8.5 / 100) × 15 / 100 ≈ 0.19, rounded to 0.2. This is negligible. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (1850 mg per 100 g; 277.5 mg per 15 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit XO sauce to no more than one teaspoon (approximately 5 g) per meal, as even a standard 15 g serving provides approximately 278 mg sodium, contributing significantly toward the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (8.5 g per 100 g) and negligible glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.2)
type2_diabetes.guidance: XO sauce has a negligible effect on postprandial blood glucose due to its very low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycemic index of approximately 15; however, patients with Type 2 Diabetes should remain mindful of the high caloric density from fat (38.5 g per 100 g) and the high sodium content when managing comorbid hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (38.5 g per 100 g), high sodium (1850 mg per 100 g), and caloric density (450 kcal per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use XO sauce sparingly (no more than 5–10 g per meal) due to its high sodium content, which elevates blood pressure risk, and its high fat content; the saturated fat contribution from Jinhua ham and dried seafood should be considered within the context of total daily saturated fat limits of less than 7% of total energy intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: XO Sauce (XO醬, XO酱) is a premium Cantonese condiment originating in Hong Kong in the 1980s. The name 'XO' is borrowed from 'extra-old' cognac, signifying luxury and the use of expensive ingredients. It is made from a rich blend of roughly chopped dried scallops (conpoy), dried shrimp, Jinhua ham (a prized Chinese cured pork), red chili peppers, garlic, and oil. The ingredients are slowly simmered and fried together until the flavors intensify and the texture becomes chunky, concentrated, and deeply aromatic. The resulting sauce is umami-rich, savory, spicy, and carries a pronounced dried seafood fragrance. Per 100 g, XO sauce provides approximately 450 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 38.5 g fat, and 8.5 g carbohydrate, with a very high sodium content of 1850 mg. It is an excellent source of vitamin B12 (3.5 mcg), selenium (45.5 mcg), and phosphorus (210 mg), reflecting its dried seafood base. XO sauce is used as a high-end condiment for dim sum dishes, a flavoring agent for stir-fried rice and noodles, and as a premium dipping sauce. It is considered one of the most prestigious Chinese condiments. Clinically, XO sauce is low-glycemic and suitable for blood sugar management in Type 2 Diabetes when used in small quantities, but its extremely high sodium content makes it a significant concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for low-sodium diets. It is not halal due to the presence of pork (Jinhua ham) and not vegetarian or vegan due to its seafood and pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for XO sauce exists in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for dried scallops, dried shrimp, cured pork, chili, garlic, and oil, weighted by typical XO sauce formulation. Input record values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for dried scallops, dried shrimp, Jinhua ham (approximated by cured pork/ham), red chili peppers, garlic, and vegetable oil. No single approved-source entry for XO sauce exists as of 2024. Input record values are consistent with this estimation and are retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for XO sauce are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024 for this specific preparation. 3. saturated_fat_g (5.8 g per 100 g) is estimated from the Jinhua ham and dried seafood fat fractions using USDA SR Legacy component data. 4. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate seafood condiments; no direct GI measurement for XO sauce exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 15 g serving ≈ 0.2 (negligible). 5. Duplicate portion entry in input record (two identical '1 serving (15g)' entries) — deduplicated to a single 15 g serving in this output. 6. is_halal set to false due to the presence of Jinhua ham (pork). 7. Ontology: FoodOn mapped to broad 'sauce (food product)' class; FoodEx2 mapped to A0FJP 'Sauces and similar products'. No species-specific ontology code exists for XO sauce in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT as of 2024.
